speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven, Mr. Andrew-- Andrew Durovecz. So you met this guy that was in the uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he spent the whole-- spent the whole night with me. And well, he said there is no time to describe how-- how the cells are-- are structured and so on. And he went on, that-- he said a plain bed-- bunk that you could sleep on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so on, the way he slept on the floor or he slept on top of that. And the next morning then-- then he started his story, that so-and-so, he's from Transylvania. He's from [NON-ENGLISH]. All those same places as [NON-ENGLISH]. But I didn't know it at that time, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he doesn't know how to go home, and that he's a-- just has no-- no chance. He has no money, how to go back home to [NON-ENGLISH]. So-- and-- and if I could give him a-- give him an address where he can-- he can put up. When he said that, the penny was dropped. And I didn't show it. And I know I didn't show it. Because there was no-- no reaction. What-- I said, I don't know Budapest. They-- they just brought me here. I lied, that, right through. And they believed it right through, that-- that the first place I was captured in-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I never got into the Budapest. And I got away with it, by God. And then I know nothing and he introduces himself. And my name is George. Well, my name is Andrew. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he introduce himself? By what name?

speaker: interviewee
text: George.

speaker: interviewer
text: No last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, just-- just George. And I'm Andrew. I said, yeah, you are a prisoner and I'm a prisoner. I didn't show. But we were trained. We were trained, you see? That-- and when asking for an address, now, my God. And-- and mind you, later on Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] was arrested. But not-- not-- not-- I thought there was an incident. She gave me a Hungarian map. And the name, you know when you get it, these kind of things that carry it with you on-- on the-- causes and so on, you would write Raymond. And when they captured me, they found that name, Raymond. And I think that was the reason-- that was the reason they found that name. And it didn't take them very much to trace Mrs.-- Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] though. And I thought it was some kind of a betrayal or something. But-- but no, [NON-ENGLISH] had-- had nothing to do with-- with that. But anyway, that was a month later. And then on my-- on my research, and all that, I met people who were-- especially the one who was a prisoner himself, a young boy, who was about the same age as Hannah, but they-- they-- they used him as a-- as a cleaner. Swept the cleaning in the-- in the-- in the prison. And I was able to contact him. And then, when I squeezed him, he-- he remembered something that he saw, this [NON-ENGLISH] around Hannah. Somewhere on the-- on the-- on the corridors. Because then how her-- her mother was-- was arrested. And so-- and then what I was able to put together, the poor girl who walked into his trap because first by the side, he knew that-- she knew that he was Jewish. He was at party-- he was in the Partisan during the Partisan. So I can proceed. He must be a comrade, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And this put together, he probably ask her where are you from? From Budapest. He probably did 18-- 18 [INAUDIBLE]. I thought her mother's the mother because-- because then, as this boy said, some-- some things-- some things had happened there that when Hannah was in-- in an interrogation, the son came. And a few hours later, her mother was in already.

speaker: interviewee
text: She so-- so she must have trusted this rat. And-- and they acted just-- and in two hour's time the Gestapo had arrested her. Horror. And but she was-- she was then-- she was then really-- she was messed up. And according to her mother's own-- own-- own words, that she was shocked. She couldn't say a word. But she was a strong woman also. And she-- she had just embraced the girl. But then they were separated right away. And-- and it was part of a torture, you see. Now, you understand, if you're face to face, mother and daughter, daughter and mother, now you're going to talk. But-- but both were solid. Solid as steel. Real conscious people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you? Did this man disappear from the-- from your cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, yes, yes. Then how-- how we met, it was some-- some 15 years later. It just--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, don't-- don't jump to the 15 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage in the present? What happened to you? Oh, oh. Oh. Because of the-- because of the nearness-- the nearness of the Red Army. It was so close that we could hear-- we could hear the artillery, boom, boom, already. We could hear the-- the-- the air bombings already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- at the moment I was up in interrogation. And they started their interrogation. Their interrogation on me at about 10:30 in the morning. And all of a sudden, this Rose guy, the tall guy, he just broke right-- right into the-- into the room-- through-- through that room. They had to go through to the-- to the colonel's, room who was the-- then the chief there. And then in a few minutes, the interrogator who interrogated me right at that moment, that captain just pushed me in the corner. And in the excitement, they just forgot about me. Completely forget about me. They got so, so, so scared. And I-- I overheard some things that-- that this Rose was talking about the Red Army, the Red Army. I was all oh, oh by God, here is my-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Do whatever you want with me, but here's-- here's my-- my salvation, the Red Army. Red Army, see? So I was there about two hours, running back and forth and so on. By the time they took me into-- into my cell, and I saw smoke go-- going up and I had to jump to grab the bar and pull, pull myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw a big pile of papers and papers, all that burning in the-- you know, all the-- all the prison documents, they were burning-- burning up. And I thought, well, here's the time they nearly-- but it wasn't. Then we spent a-- spent a night-- we were another night there. And the next day, they put me up with [NON-ENGLISH] the other group. The first, the captain, and another lieutenant, they were-- they were shipped out first into [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a-- it was a summer resort in the Buda-- in the Buda Hills. And then it was converted into a prisoner's-- no, not a prisoner's camp, but a-- an escape is what-- the Polish army, when Hitler invaded Poland, some-- some 120,000 of the Polish Army's officers escaped, and Hungary took them in because there's the ancient centuries of relationships between Hungary-- Hungary, who had-- and they put them into this-- into this summer resort camp. And but then-- then they let them-- they dispersed them in-- in the country and in Budapest. But they still had an office there, where they picked up. They-- they-- they-- relief, really a few-- few [NON-ENGLISH] there and so on. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you go there, this tall man that you were talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the-- he was the chief interrogator.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- did you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose. The very next day, very next day when I was put into-- into cell four, in that prison, that Budapest one, they-- the prisoners-- the-- the prisoners-- no, the guards. The-- the guards, the NCO's, the Non-Commissioned Officer, when I was let out to the-- to the toilet, he-- he told me. There were good guys. He told me, watch out this wild beast. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his name was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Rose. Rose, a rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this his real name? You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. But there, in the prison, I-- I know that-- that he was-- he was known under the name of Rose. And it could've been his real name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because was in the counter intelligence, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the pre-interview, you mentioned something, that you are suspecting that one of the interrogators was somebody else. I am not exactly sure. But you-- you were talking to me about another person that-- whose name is very famous. And I'm not-- I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't-- I don't recall, unless-- unless you misunderstood me on-- on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on something. Because I'm-- I'm-- I know I'm going too fast, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But where I was interrogated was several-- several people in Miskolc. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here. And there were some who just-- we just went and went over and over where I was born. And I had to give-- I had to give the exact location. Like in Miskolc, they brought in a professor, a university professor of Miskolc University, who spoke English. And they just left us in the-- in the middle, right in the middle, two chairs, facing like this. And he spoke-- spoke to me in English. And turned out to be he was-- he was an employee of the Hungarian Ministry of Agriculture He was sent out to Canada, to Canadian plains to study agriculture, especially with Canadian weed growing and so on. And he knew the place, [NON-ENGLISH], where-- in a place that I-- I that was in my cover story, that-- that I was born in [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I was an organizer for a moment in before the war. And I know-- knew the area. I knew the people. And-- and my cover story, that my-- my father was born in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's-- let's-- let's go back to-- to

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To this time when you-- you were getting towards the end of your prison days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they-- then they took us-- [NON-ENGLISH], the other Canadian-Hungarian and myself, and put us into-- into a-- a motorcycle with the-- with the side. What do you call them? And I was sitting in-- on a seat, Mike, sitting on my lap. And there-- there was a-- the driver of the-- the motorcycle, and this-- this officer who was escorting us up to-- up to [NON-ENGLISH], in the-- in the-- in the Buda mountains. There we-- we found-- we found an English captain. We found a-- a-- a Soviet major and a Romanian lieutenant. And the two-- two British, and the two of us, there. And well, from that, one by one, we were taken-- taken away. One every day. Every day we arrived there. On Friday afternoon, early Friday-- Friday afternoon. So Friday, Saturday, Sunday, came Monday. And Monday night, with the help of the Polish underground, where they an inroad into the camp at about 10:10 on a-- on a Monday. You want to-- yeah, yeah, Monday evening, with his help, I escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Budapest. We went through to-- to a guard. There was just a guard with-- with one rifle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and he knew the Polish, you see, that they had a permit to come and going in, in and out. And when we were going, wherever God set his heart. Or-- and as well, mister, you weren't in the know. And who is this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, I-- I forget to tell you that I-- I had an order to take this-- to take this for a friend, out. And then he told me, don't-- don't-- don't dare to speak Hungarian, or-- or-- or any-- any other language. Not even-- not even in English. Don't say-- don't say nothing. But he-- he was just-- and then he did this and that. And so-- and just, he winked at me, and I knew. Because he, when he came in, he brought a loaded pistol. And I already had it. And I had my hat on-- my hand on it. And when he winked, I just pulled out and right in the small-- small head. And-- and he just collapsed. And we just walked out. Not far-- not far, there-- there was a German streetcar. Boarded the streetcar in about five minutes. And into the-- into the chapel. There was still, on the Buda side, in a [NON-ENGLISH] chapel there. And there they were waiting for me already. Behind the altar, there were six-- six or seven people. So this Polish lead me in-- in there. And there was a group of resistance. And so the priest-- the head priest of the-- of the chapel, and some-- some of the monks. And the very first thing-- thing was that-- that-- that the Germans already-- already mined our beautiful bridges, the six bridges on the Danube. The Pearl of the Danube. And we are informed-- see, that's how I said we are informed that you are-- you are trained on demolition and this kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can you help us? How can dismantle and-- and save the-- the bridges of Budapest. So they-- Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. my-- my good friends, I was trained how to blow them up, not to save. But-- but I have some knowledge, how to dismantle them. But I don't know you. I know you-- the dynamite, the black powder, or whatever. I work with explosive the Germans use.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me that all connected into one, one place, right into this chapel here, that the command-- this demolition command is-- is right-- right-- right-- right above there. There was a-- a convent for-- for the-- woman, what you call them? Nuns.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nuns, yeah. That they were evacuated. And that's where they said, but-- I said, well, first I didn't believe them, that it was all-- all connected there. And I thought I told them, well to me it sounds impossible. But from that distance, to bring all those-- no. Every-- every bridge is-- is mined separately and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But even to save one, give me-- give me about three, four days to think it-- think it over, what we will need-- need more information if I can get it. And they told me where-- where this friend who brought me over from the-- from the camp, he will give you all the information. OK? And then-- and then when-- then he led me after-- after this.

speaker: interviewee
text: We shook hands. I never saw a face. They never saw my face. and then from-- from there, we walked, which was only about 10 minutes walk. We have to up in the, in the, once, into-- into [NON-ENGLISH]. 18 [NON-ENGLISH] Youth there. They were waiting for us already. But they-- they were waiting for artists also. They were waiting for the Soviet major. But he had to-- he had to tell me. But he told me, don't speak Hungarian. Let-- let me do all the talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: English. Because he told me, you can speak English there. And a young lady, she was a-- she was a divorcee, she spoke fluent in English. And the whole family, except the-- except the old man, he was an ex-- ex-- ex-- Secretary of State of Agriculture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was-- he was retired. But he didn't speak any-- any English. But then only-- only on the third day, then when he told me I was invited for dinner. He was also visiting me. And then when I came into the-- into the [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS] That was it. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you revealed that you were speaking Hungarian, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And then all-- whole things changed. And then-- then-- I was then started-- started operation [INAUDIBLE]. And too much-- too much details what-- what we have done. Not very much, but we were able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were able to dismantle the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I told them, look, for this kind of operation, even to save one-- one bridge, we need at least 20 well-balanced, well trained men who can handle arms, short arms, pistols in the [INAUDIBLE] and so on, who had to know the exact spot where the-- the-- the explosive is placed and this kind of thing. And when I told him how-- how it was, because it is universal, well, he-- he practically caved in, you see? That it takes all this, and so on. Because we-- we had to disarm. We have to, if possible, silently disarm. Silent killing. dagger, and so. Because you can't do shooting, going around at the-- at the bridge heads and so on, to get rid of the German-- German guard. And then find what you call-- And that's-- and it is my job, where and how to cut the-- cut the wires, to be saved, not to explode it. But to neutralize it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Durovecz, during this time in Budapest, did you have any knowledge of what the Germans were doing with the Jewish people? Did you meet any Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not-- not at this moment. But the very evening that I was taken into the [NON-ENGLISH] house-- into the [NON-ENGLISH] house, the second floor was an exclusive quarter for the son of the-- of the family, [NON-ENGLISH] Jr. He was a-- he was a-- he-- he was a lawyer. And when-- when I was in-- I-- I was introduced, we shook hand with [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a Jewish fellow. He was a friend of-- a friend of [NON-ENGLISH].

words.text: He was a-- he was a-- he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he slept upstairs. And so we slept in the adjoining-- adjoining-- the rooms upstairs. Because he said what-- and no family you have-- what you call them-- could-- could-- it was like-- like a neighbor-- neighbor's house. Well, it was-- it was a villa, you see? And they build it. They could afford it. And so-- Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: But [NON-ENGLISH] was-- his friend and-- but mind you, he spent almost six months in the attic. And [NON-ENGLISH] used to steal from the kitchen. it was literally food, and take him up. Finally, after months, his mother caught him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, his parents didn't know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody knew. Except the maid. She knows because she had to go-- go up there when they washed the-- that's where they hung their laundry and so on. But she kept quiet. [NON-ENGLISH] told me, and-- and she kept quiet. But then one time a [NON-ENGLISH] was carrying after the supper, when everybody went to the dining room. He just grabbed him a plate, and walking out, and mother came in back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the end of it were saying, God, you [INAUDIBLE], of what you do everything? And then he had to-- to admit it. We were exposed. And right away, they brought him-- brought him down and took him into the family at this point. During this time there were protected housing in Budapest for Jewish people. And all kinds of operations going on there. Did you hear anything from your friends? From--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: yeah. Well, I haven't heard much yet. But-- but some people came into the house, where we were visiting the house. And they talked about right, some-- some friends of the-- of the family, they did try to escape to Vienna. They tried-- they tried to break-- to break out of the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there they were caught by the Red Army. See? They had to come back. And they destroyed the Red-- all the Germans-- the Germans there, on the Budapest to Vienna road at the end of the city. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And these people, their relatives tried to escape with the-- with the-- with the Germans. And they came back. And then they-- by the time they were away, Partisans occupied their house. But not now. But-- but there-- there were only-- only a few-- few people together, you see, who were-- who were doing some-- some-- some sabotage. And the-- the-- saving, mostly-- the big task was save the Jews. And right across from 19 around 20, to 21, 24, [NON-ENGLISH] youth till 24. The-- the Swedish legation was there. And they had three buildings there. One where they had all offices. And one was almost right across-- right across from us. And we have heard it from-- from a-- these were the soldier who were-- who were around. And a-- a wounded Hungarian soldier, and they deserted-- deserted there. He told something he knew, something in the family. And Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] was a-- was a real devil. She-- she was a courageous sense a woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're saying devil on a-- on a positive-- in a positive way.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, in her manner. This-- she was brave and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told me, but what will you do, the winter? And then-- then I just told her look, [NON-ENGLISH], look. It's not-- not far to go. Dress up and pull the-- pull the-- pull the-- cap over your head, and so on, that your face doesn't show much. And let's-- let's scout it, see what's-- what's on the other side. And sure enough, we were able to sneak into the huge yard, you see. It used to be one of those big villas and the Buda villas. And we got into the buildings. And as we arrived at the door, there was the-- there was a nurse there. Only-- only four people there. And we counted [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and there was no sense of counting. About 120 small Jewish children, the oldest-- oldest were about two years old, hardly-- hardly any-- any that were three years old. All very small, this was small children. And they didn't even ask how they got them here. There were these people there. There were four of them, three of them, in-- in a sort, were nurses or some in nurses' uniform. But in another quarter there was the-- the-- the-- the-- the smell was just deadly. Hungarian wounded soldiers, unattended. Some of them, their wound was rotting already. There, but they were jammed out. All this was one-- one huge basement. And they were on the floor, on the spread out-- spread out straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but these children--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were blankets. And there were all these children there. You could imagine, you see it. And food, whoever they could get to get it and steal food. And-- and smuggled it-- smuggled that in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they collected most of-- of these children. I have a book. Unfortunately, it's in-- in Hungarian. They are [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So mostly-- mostly woman charity organization, religious. Including Catholic woman's organization. And Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] herself was the-- was the-- the chair of the-- of the-- Cattolica. The woman's branch of the Cattolica. So there's this-- the woman's organization, national woman's organization of the-- of the Catholic-- Catholic woman. She was the national chairman. And so she-- she helped in-- where were they-- that they took-- took them-- took them into monasteries and-- and many, many other places. I-- I-- I have some in the book. But and they trade union [NON-ENGLISH] and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where-- the-- the parents were-- were driven away, into-- into the-- into yellow cross-- yellow star houses. It wasn't [NON-ENGLISH] house. But it was the yellow star houses where-- where they ghettoed them all around. Or-- or drove them away. The-- the [NON-ENGLISH] people, men and-- men and women into the German underground, underground factories, war factories. And so-- and the children's were left behind. And there-- there were people who gathered them, all-- all kinds of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they took care of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they took-- took care of them. In the church basement, or the church, or the schools, and trade union halls and such organizations. And there were-- there were-- many, many of them were saved-- saved this-- this way. And then we had a cow. Because [NON-ENGLISH] family, being a-- a minister and so on, they had a small little farm. And just a small farm and a few cows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just before-- just before-- just before the-- the city was encircled, the men we had out there, they formed And so led the cow into-- into the city and put-- put the cow into the gutters because the garage was-- was confiscated by the Germans. You see? So and he brought enough-- enough hay-- hay and that there was still milk to deliver, that we have milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even that we took over then, for the children. Then-- then came a-- came a, a condition situation what of you couldn't even-- even get-- get close to it.

words.text: Then-- then came a-- came a,

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed with the [NON-ENGLISH] family until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was right through. Right through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till what date? When was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The raid-- the last-- last day was when I left the hosts in February 15. Well now '40-- that was in '45. Or that was the time where-- when the last battle took place on Sunday, Sunday afternoon, and Sunday-- Sunday night. And Monday morning, it was at 10 o'clock came the Soviet blitz. Spreading the city with leaflets. [CRYING] And greeting the city, you are free. Be calm and so on. And start to-- to readjust and restart your life and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and-- the-- at the end of the following day, there came-- came a-- a-- a Red Army patrol with a Hungarian man with a-- with a red arm band and so. And everybody from the age of 16 to 60, we were driven out to dig out a-- a tank. In those Buda Hills, you see, the-- the-- the Nazis dug-- dug a tank trap, as they called, a tank trap. And-- and the Soviets put railway tracks. But they were too short. And it was for heavy tank. The [NON-ENGLISH] tanks went on. And they [INAUDIBLE]. But-- and then we had to help to-- to dig out. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now. And we'll continue.
